coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection for uric acid using bifunctional carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fan
Chen, Jin
Wen, Jiayi
Peng, Yiyang
Tang, Xiaomin
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Iron, cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FeCo/N-CDs) were fabricated firstly by a one pot hydrothermal method in this study. As well as exhibiting peroxidase-like activity, the prepared FeCo/N-CDs emitted fluorescence at 475 nm upon the excitation of 360 nm. A dual-signal biosensor relied on ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric method was developed for detecting uric acid (UA) based on FeCo/N-CDs-catalyzed oxidation of OPD. Uricase catalyzed the production of H2O2 from UA, and FeCo/N-CDs catalyzed H2O2 into the •OH radicals, contributing to the oxidation of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) from o-phenylenediamine (OPD). The color from colorless to yellow was observed and DAP can quench the fluorescence of FeCo/N-CDs via the inner filter effect (IFE), accompanied with a significant fluorescence enhancement of DAP at 580 nm. The fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption intensities were thus affected by sequential additions of UA. Quantitative analysis of UA was based on the fluorescence intensity of DAP/FeCo/N-CDs (I 580/I 475) and color records. The calibration curve was linear within the range of 0.2–150 μM and the limit of detection was low as 0.05 μM. The biosensor was successfully applied to the detection of UA in serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uric acid
Uricase
Bifunction
FeCo/N-CDs
O-phenylenediamine
Serum and urine samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of UA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China. The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants. The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FeCo/N-CDs, inset: the HRTEM image; (b) Size distribution histogram of FeCo/N-CDs; (c) FT-IR spectra of FeCo/N-CDs; (d) XPS analysis of FeCo/N-CDs, inset: atomic percentage of elements in FeCo/N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra for FeCo/N-CDs with excitation wavelengths between 300 and 380 nm; (b) UV–vis absorption (blue curve), excitation (red curve), and emission spectra (black curve) of FeCo/N-CDs; (c) The relationship between fluorescent intensity and FeCo/N-CDs concentration.

The absorbance evolution over reaction time (a) at UV–vis spectra of the biosensor at different OPD levels and its Lineweaver-Burk plot (b); the absorbance evolution over reaction time (c) at UV–vis spectra of the biosensor at different H2O2 levels and its Lineweaver-Burk plot (d). The amout of FeCo/N-CDs was 70 μL.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of DAP and emission spectrum of N-CDs; (b) The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of FeCo/N-CDs with and without DAP; (c) The zeta potentials of FeCo/N-CDs and DAP.
(a) Fluorescence spectrum and (b) UV–vis spectrum of various interactions between FeCo/N-CDs and different substances. Conditions: UA, 80 μM; uricase, 4 μg mL−1; FeCo/N-CDs, 70 μL; OPD, 5 mM.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation of 360 nm (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon ultraviolet light), and (c) UV–vis spectra (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon daylight) of the biosensor with different UA levels (from down to up: 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 70, 100, 150 μM); (b) The linear relationship between ΔA (at 450 nm) and UA concentrations; (d) The linear relationship between I 580/I 475 and UA concentrations.

Selectivity of the sensor for both fluorescence (a) and UV–vis absorption (b) detection (grey columns: in the absence of UA, red columns: in the presence of UA). Each letter stands for a different substance: (a) NaCl, (b) KCl, (c) CaCl2, (d) NH4Cl, (e) AA, (f) cystine, (g) urea, (h) dopamine, (i) glycine, (j) glucose, (k) creatinine, (l) UA. The concentration of UA, AA and cystine was 30 μM, 10 μM and 10 μM, respectively. The concentration of other substances was 300 μM.
Schematic illustration of the biosensor based on FeCo/N-CDs as a peroxidase mimic and fluorescent probe.
Fluorescence and UV–vis determination of UA in human serum samples (n = 3).
Fluorescence and UV–vis determination of UA in human urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection for uric acid using bifunctional carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron, cobalt and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FeCo/N-CDs) were fabricated firstly by a one pot hydrothermal method in this study. As well as exhibiting peroxidase-like activity, the prepared FeCo/N-CDs emitted fluorescence at 475 nm upon the excitation of 360 nm. A dual-signal biosensor relied on ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric method was developed for detecting uric acid (UA) based on FeCo/N-CDs-catalyzed oxidation of OPD. Uricase catalyzed the production of H2O2 from UA, and FeCo/N-CDs catalyzed H2O2 into the •OH radicals, contributing to the oxidation of 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) from o-phenylenediamine (OPD). The color from colorless to yellow was observed and DAP can quench the fluorescence of FeCo/N-CDs via the inner filter effect (IFE), accompanied with a significant fluorescence enhancement of DAP at 580 nm. The fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption intensities were thus affected by sequential additions of UA. Quantitative analysis of UA was based on the fluorescence intensity of DAP/FeCo/N-CDs (I 580/I 475) and color records. The calibration curve was linear within the range of 0.2–150 μM and the limit of detection was low as 0.05 μM. The biosensor was successfully applied to the detection of UA in serum and urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2,6,8-trihydroxypurine, UA) is the primary final product from purine derivatives as a result of human metabolism. UA level in humans is a significant biomarker of numerous disease states, with the normal level in serum at 0.12 − 0.45 mM and in urine at 1.4 − 4.4 mM [1,2]. Oversupply of UA in biological fluids could cause gout, cardiovascular disease, Lesh-Nyhan syndrome, obesity, hyperuricemia whereas lower concentrations of UA contribute to multiple sclerosis and diabetes mellitus [3,4]. Hence, a rapid and accurate method is urgent for real-time monitoring of UA levels in serum and urine samples.

Thus far, various methods toward the detection of UA have been reported, such as high performance liquid chromatography, fluorescence spectroscopy, electrochemistry, colorimetry [5–8], etc. Of these, fluorescence spectroscopy presents high sensitivity, simplicity of tools, rapid analysis and ease of use [9]. In particular, ratiometric fluorescence analysis involves the simultaneous measurement of two optical signals with one excitation wavelength, thus reducing false signals arising from environment interference and improving accuracy and sensitivity [10]. And the colorimetry could provide a fast determination of UA level by using its color system visible to the naked eye [11]. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-read assay for UA can unify the advantages of both, so as to obtain a satisfactory detection method. The results of this approach are more convincing than those of conventional single-signal sensing platforms.

Since UA can produce H2O2 under the catalysis of uricase, a color-change based catalyzed reaction between H2O2 and horseradish peroxidase to oxidize OPD (colorless) and generate DAP (yellow) was noted. On the other hand, fluorescence is absent in OPD, but highly present in DAP [12]. By interacting the optical signal generated by the fluorescent materials and the optical signal generated by the DAP, a ratiometric fluorescence strategy can be constructed [13]. But considering that horseradish peroxidase is easily inactivated and costly, peroxidase mimics appear to be the best alternative. To improve this problem, the material in our study was expected to be not only fluorescent, but also peroxidase-like active.

Comparatively to other nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely utilized in biochemical analysis, cell imaging, and other fields due to their simple preparation procedure, large surface areas, and highly intense luminescence [14–16]. But few reports were available regarding bifunctional CDs with peroxidase-like and luminescence properties. Heteroatom chemical doping can change the internal electronic environment of CDs, endow CDs with new functions and broaden its application field [17,18]. According to recent reports, doping with transition metal ions can benefit CDs by giving them a strong catalytic activity and structural stability, which results from metals being coordinated to CDs. Among them, iron and cobalt are the ideal doping elements among them [19]. Furthermore, the surface of CDs can be passivated by doping CDs with amine-functional molecules, thus improving its optical properties and high fluorescence quantum yield [20].

In response to the promising optical properties of Fe, Co, N co-doped CDs (FeCo/N-CDs) in biosensing and peroxidase-like activity, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric approach for UA with high sensitivity and high selectivity. Based on the reactions mentioned above and upon increasing the concentration of UA, the color of the biosensor system gradually changed to yellow. Moreover, the visible fluorescence of FeCo/N-CDs at 475 nm was gradually quenched by DAP via the inner filter effect (IFE), while the fluorescence of DAP at 580 nm intensified accordingly. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I 580/I 475 could serve as an indicator for UA detection. Additionally, the dual-read method for quantifying UA was successfully utilized in the analysis and on-site monitoring of human serum and urine samples.

50 μL of UA standard solutions of varying concentrations were mixed with 50 μL of uricase (80 μg·mL−1) in the 100 μL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 8.5). Stabilization of the mixture was carried out at 35 °C for 15 min in order to produce H2O2. Then the resulting solution was transferred into the mixture including 630 μL acetate buffer solution (0.1 mM, pH 5), 100 μL OPD (30 mM) and 70 μL FeCo/N-CDs. Further incubation at 40 °C was allowed for 20 min. Fluorescence spectra at 360 nm excitation and the absorption spectra at 450 nm were collected, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: As an application of the proposed biosensor, it was used to quantitate UA in human serum and urine samples. Freshly-collected serum samples of human subjects were provided by Nanchang University Fourth Affiliated Hospital (Nanchang, China). The serum samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and were diluted in 8 times by phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 8.5) for further use. Urine samples from two volunteers were collected in the morning and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was taken and filtered with 0.22 µm Milli-pore filter. The filtrate obtained was diluted 40 times by PBS solution for reserve.

Biochemical analysis method (Olympus AU2700) was also used to compare the results of the dual-read system. The centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 3 min preceded the biochemical analysis of the serum samples. Each of the supernatants (5.6 μL) was enriched with a combination of uricase, 4-aminoantipyrine, and 3-diphenylamine disodium salt. Analyzing the mixtures at 660/800 nm was followed by biochemical analysis.

FeCo/N-CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal route (the detailed process was in the Supplementary Material). The morphology of the prepared FeCo/N-CDs was characterized via TEM measurements. As presented in TEM images ( Fig. 1a), the FeCo/N-CDs showed a uniformly spherical shape with a few of nanometers across. From particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b), it was found that their average size was about 2.1 nm. As can be seen in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a, inset), FeCo/N-CDs exhibited ordered lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of the graphitic [21]. Analysis of the surface composition was conducted using FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra revealed the stretching vibrations of O-H at 3339 cm−1, CO at 1839 cm−1, CC at 1711 cm−1, C−N at 1334 cm−1 and C−O at 1017 cm−1, showing the presence of multiple functional groups on the surface of FeCo/N-CDs. Beyond that, the spectrum also showed a series of small absorption peaks in the metallic sensitive region of 871 − 658 cm−1, possibly matching N-Fe-N and N-Co-N bonds [22]. The further analysis towards chemical composition of FeCo/N-CDs was conducted by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1d, the spectrum of FeCo/N-CDs included peaks for C 1 s (287.1 eV), N 1 s (399.5 eV), O 1 s (533.0 eV), Fe 2p (711.1 eV) and Co 2p (787.0 eV), by which it could be calculated to have 65.9 % of carbon, 5.6 % of nitrogen, 24.1 % of oxygen, 2.4 % of iron, and 2.0 % of cobalt elements. Based on the C1s spectrum (Fig. S1a), CO, C−O, C−N, and C−C bonds were characterized by binding energies of 288, 286.8, 285.4, and 284.4 eV respectively [23]. Fe 2p spectra (Fig. S1c) showed two main peaks at 722.8 eV and 709.3 eV with satellite peaks. Co 2p spectrum (Fig. S1d) featured four typical peaks at 800.5 eV, 796.2 eV, 784.3 eV and 780.1 eV. Also, valence state of Co atoms in FeCo/N-CDs was mostly + 2 and + 3, which indicated that Co3+ was not completely reduced during the preparation process. Two peaks were observed at 399.6 eV and 400.7 eV in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1b) which were attributed to CN − H and C−N, respectively [24]. All these results indicated there has been success in preparing FeCo/N-CDs.

The blue emitting FeCo/N-CDs exhibit special fluorescence properties. With the excitation wavelength varying from 300 to 380 nm, the position of the fluorescence peak at 475 nm remained almost invariant ( Fig. 2a), revealing that FeCo/N-CDs exhibited an excitation-independent emission property. It is most likely that FeCo/N-CDs were driven by one edge transition mode in the fluorescence process, which caused excitation-independent emission [25]. A maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained for excitation at 360 nm. Fig. 2b showed UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue curve), emission spectrum (black curve) and excitation spectrum (red curve) of the bifunctional CDs. It was found that a pair of UV–visible absorption peaks were detected at 246 nm and 345 nm originated from π − π* transition in the sp2 carbon of CO band and n − π* transition in the CN band, respectively [22]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the fluorescence emission intensity at 475 nm increased in proportion to the increase in bifunctional CDs. It was concluded that FeCo/N-CDs have good stability and dispersion in solution. The quantum yield of FeCo/N-CDs was 35.33 %, while that of Fe, Co co-doped CDs was 2.39 %, measured using a photoluminescence spectrometer equipped with integrating sphere (Fig. S2). It demonstrated the remarkable performance of the synthesized FeCo/N-CDs in terms of high fluorescence quantum yield.

By studying the catalytic performance of FeCo/N-CDs for H2O2 oxidation of OPD, the peroxide-like catalytic activity was investigated. As Fig. S3 showed, when FeCo/N-CDs was absent, the system was colorless. However, the solution containing H2O2 turned yellow when FeCo/N-CDs was present. In addition, the absorption intensity at 450 nm increased with the increase of H2O2 concentration, accompanied by the color changes from colorless to bright yellow. These experiments indicated that FeCo/N-CDs has peroxide-like catalytic activity, and can catalyze the oxidation of OPD by H2O2.

In order to further explore the activity of peroxidase, the steady-state kinetics of OPD oxidation catalyzed by FeCo/N-CDs was tested. So we analyzed the absorbance (at 450 nm) evolution over reaction time at UV–vis spectra of the biosensor at different H2O2 levels and at different OPD levels respectively ( Fig. 3a and c), by which their Lineweaver−Burk plots can be obtained (Fig. 3b and d). For FeCo/N-CDs toward OPD, the Michaelis constant (K m) and maximal reaction velocity (V max) were calculated to be 1.39 mM and 2.07 μM/min, respectively. The small value of K m indicated the high binding affinity of FeCo/N-CDs for H2O2, whereas the great value of V max indicated a high catalytic activity of FeCo/N-CDs. On the other hand, K m and V max were 0.3 mM and 16.91 μM/min toward H2O2, respectively. Compared with the previous literatures [26–28], FeCo/N-CDs have a much lower K m value towards H2O2 than those for Ru−C3N4 (2.4 mM), horseradish peroxidase (0.34 mM), and Co3O4 (0.61 mM), which showed that the prepared FeCo/N-CDs have a high affinity for H2O2. To sum up, the functional CDs synthesized possess highly catalytic activity.

Scheme 1 illustrates the working principle of the proposed dual-read sensing system using FeCo/N-CDs·H2O2 was produced by allantoin production from UA catalyzed by uricase and H2O2 can be efficiently catalyzed by FeCo/N-CDs to yield •OH radicals. As a result of the oxidation of OPD to DAP with •OH radicals, the system solution was subsequently converted to a yellow color. On the flip side, when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, FeCo/N-CDs could emit intense blue fluorescence with a peak wavelength at 475 nm. However, the oxidic products DAP could quench the fluorescence at 475 nm and generate yellow fluorescence with a peak wavelength at 580 nm. The fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption intensities were significantly correlated with UA level. Finally, determination of UA concentration was based on the fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP/FeCo/N-CDs (I 580/I 475) and the absorbance at 450 nm.

In order to clarify the fluorescence quenching mechanism between FeCo/N-CDs and DAP, a series of experiments followed. In Fig. 4a, the absorption spectrum of DAP near 450 nm and the emission spectrum of FeCo/N-CDs near 475 nm exhibited a large spectral overlap, indicating either internal filtering effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) was responsible [29]. To investigate further, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes of FeCo/N-CDs with and without DAP as shown in Fig. 4b. It was observed there was no significant variation of fluorescence lifetime curves in both cases, indicating that there was no energy transfer between FeCo/N-CDs and DAP. Subsequently, DAP and FeCo/N-CDs showed zeta potential of −38.9 mV and −35.2 mV respectively in Fig. 4c. As both Zeta potentials were negative with electrostatic repulsion, FeCo/N-CDs and DAP would be further apart than 10 nm [30]. According to report in reference, a minimum distance of 10 nm between the fluorescence donor and the receptor is a requirement for FRET [31]. Thus, the quenching of FeCo/N-CDs by DAP could only be owe to IFE.

The influence of relevant factors in the system was carried out for the feasibility investigation of the biosensor for UA detection. As seen in the fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 5a), curves a-f did not change significantly when FeCo/N-CDs was mixed with different substance combinations alone under the same conditions. Only when four main components (FeCo/N-CDs, UA, uricase, and OPD) coexisted in the system, the fluorescence quenching would appear at 475 nm, and an emerging emission peak would be observed at 580 nm (curve g). Consequently, all these substances contributed significantly to quantitative analysis for UA, but none of them affected the results individually. In addition, there was no new peak at 580 nm (curve c), which suggested that pure OPD solution did not lead it. Only an emerging emission peak would be observed at 580 nm (curve g), it could be a new substance (DAP) from UA and uricase interacted with FeCo/N-CDs and OPD together to cause the phenomenon. In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 5b), only curve g had an obvious peak at 450 nm, that was featured by the strongest absorption peak of DAP. It revealed that there was no DAP formation in the other six systems and FeCo/N-CDs showed peroxidase-like activity. It was worth noting that the absorption spectrum of FeCo/N-CDs had no absorption peak around 450 nm. Further, FeCo/N-CDs was traceable within the system and did not affect UV–vis spectra for UA detection. Thus, the proposed method can be successfully applied to the analysis of UA.

At the optimal conditions described at Supplementary Material, linear range and limit of detection were explored to demonstrate the performance of the biosensor for quantitating UA. In the presence of increasing UA concentrations, the fluorescent color changed from blue to yellow, which translated to a progressive decrease in emission intensity at 475 nm whereas an increase in emission intensity at 580 nm ( Fig. 6a). Meanwhile, the color of the solution gradually turned yellow from colorless observed by naked eye under day light. And the absorbance at 450 nm in the UV–vis spectra also increased accordingly (Fig. 6c).

In Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 580/I 475) displayed linear correlations with the corresponding UA concentrations between 0.2 and 150 μM, with a linear regression equation of I 580/I 475 = 0.00425 CUA+ 0.18148 (R2 =0.994). According to 3σ/s (s was the slope of the curve while σ was the standard deviation), the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.05 μМ. Another calibration plot was produced by UV–vis absorption spectra for the sensing platform as well (Fig. 6d). ΔA (ΔA = A−A0, A0 and A represented the absorbance of the biosensor with and without UA, respectively) against their corresponding UA concentrations had a good linear relationship across a range of 0.2 − 30 μM. This resulted in a linear regression equation of ΔA = 0.00913 CUA + 0.05488 (R2 =0.995), with limit of detection of 0.12 μМ.

For evaluating the selectivity of the proposed biosensor for UA quantitation in real samples, we examined its response to common biological substances in serum and urine. Based on the ideal experimental conditions, separately, each interferer was added to the system in the presence and absence of UA, and the results were measured using I 580/I 475 and the absorbance at 450 nm. There was a normal level of cysteine was far lower than UA found in urine and serum of humans [32]. For this reason, a dose of 10 μM cysteine was chosen. Typically, UA was much more abundant in body fluids than ascorbic acid as well [33]. As a consequence, the concentration of AA for this experiment was 10 μM. In Fig. 7a and b, the results obtained were not significantly different when the system was tested with UA and different interference. But among these substances tested separately, only UA altered the biosensor in a noticeable way. To summarize, the proposed method was designed to detect UA in serum and urine with a high degree of selectivity and feasibility.

For evaluating the usefulness of the biosensor, several serum and urine samples were collected for determination of UA. The results of UA in serum detected by the biosensor were similar to those of the biochemical analyzer as shown in Table 1. For serum samples, the recoveries ranged from 90.2 % to 104 % and from 96.0 % to 101 % in the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption method, respectively. For urine samples ( Table 2), the recoveries ranged from 104 % to 110 % and from 89.5 % to 109 % in the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption method, respectively. RSD of all real samples of both techniques was no more than 3.2 %, indicated the dual-read biosensor using FeCo/N-CDs was reliable for UA detection.

In addition, it was interesting to note that as seen in Fig. S5a and S5c, the colors presented by images of the original serum and urine samples were very consistent with the color range corresponding to UA concentration in Fig. 6c. Five serum-containing systems and two urine-containing systems were visibly darker with the addition of standard UA solutions (Fig S5b and d). The results were consistent with those previously discussed. In spite of these, the application of the method must be viewed through the lens of two major limitations, such as poor specificity in strong fluorescence and UV–vis absorption backgrounds. Fortunately, the actual samples involved were then diluted by a certain multiple and there was no significant background interference in either detection technique. These results indicate that the biosensor has considerable application potential in human serum and urine samples.

Bifunctional FeCo/N-CDs with peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence properties were successfully prepared. A rapid and accurate ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection for UA was established based on the uricase-catalyzed oxidation of UA, in combination with the FeCo/N-CDs-catalyzed oxidation of OPD. This method is also low cost and convenient, because the materials required are not very expensive, and the operation is not complicated, either in the synthesis of FeCo/N-CDs or in the detection process. The color change of the biosensor with different concentrations of UA can be visually observed, making it easier for the naked eye to determine the relative concentration of UA. Furthermore, the concentration of UA was quantified by fluorescence and ultraviolet visible signal generated by the sensor. In general, the prepared FeCo/N-CDs have outstanding optical properties and catalytic activity, and the dual-read assay for quantification of UA shows a great deal of potential in terms of utility derivatization.

Fan Li: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jin Chen: Methodology, Visualization. Jiayi Wen: Validation, Data curation. Yiyang Peng: Investigation. Xiaomin Tang: Resources. Ping Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China.

ce:section.ce:para: The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132381.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of enzymatic uric acid biosensors based on amperometric detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.E. Erden, E. Kılıç, A review of enzymatic uric acid biosensors based on amperometric detection, Talanta, 107 (2013) 312–323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Baseline serum uric acid level as a predictor of cardiovascular disease related mortality and all-cause mortality: a meta-analysis of prospective studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Zhao, L. Huang, M. Song, Y. Song, Baseline serum uric acid level as a predictor of cardiovascular disease related mortality and all-cause mortality: a meta-analysis of prospective studies, Atherosclerosis, 231 (2013) 61–68.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uric acid: a marker of increased cardiovascularrisk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.C. Gagliardi, M.H. Miname, Uric acid: a marker of increased cardiovascularrisk, Atherosclerosis, 202 (2009) 11–17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Altered uric acid levels and disease states

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Kutzing, B.L. Firestein, Altered uric acid levels and disease states, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther., 324 (2008) 1–7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance liquid chromatographic isolation of uric acid from soil for isotopic determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Mizutani, Y. Kabaya, E. Wada, High-performance liquid chromatographic isolation of uric acid from soil for isotopic determination, J. Chromatogr. A, 331 (1985) 371–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and sensitive fluorescence based biosensor for the determination of uric acid using H2O2-sensitive quantum dots/dual enzymes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.E. Azmi, N.I. Ramli, J. Abdullah, M.A. A. Hamid, H. Sidek, S. Abd Rahman, A simple and sensitive fluorescence based biosensor for the determination of uric acid using H2O2-sensitive quantum dots/dual enzymes, Biosens. Bioelectron., 67 (2015) 129–133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uric acid monitoring with a smartphone as the electrochemical analyzer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.L. Medintz, A.R. Clapp, H. Mattoussi, E.R. Goldman, B. Fisher, J.M. Mauro, Self-assembled nanoscale biosensors based on quantum dot FRET donors, Nat. Mater., 2 (2003) 630–638.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Isolation of six cysteine proteinase inhibitors from human urine. Their physicochemical and enzyme kinetic properties and concentrations in biological fluids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Abrahamson, A.J. Barrett, G. Salvesen, A. Grubb, Isolation of six cysteine proteinase inhibitors from human urine. Their physicochemical and enzyme kinetic properties and concentrations in biological fluids, J. Biol. Chem., 261(1986) 11282–11289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the amount of ascorbic acid in blood and urine. The daily human requirements for ascorbic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Van Eekelen, On the amount of ascorbic acid in blood and urine. The daily human requirements for ascorbic acid, Biochem. J., 30 (1936) 2291.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fan Li received her bachelor’s degree (2019) from Jiangxi Agricultural University, China and now is a 3rd year M.D. student. Her research focuses on the design and application of biosensor for detection of uric acid.
Jin Chen received her bachelor’s degree (2021) from Jiangxi Normal University, China and now is a 1st year M.D. student. Her research focuses on catalysis performance of nano enzyme.
Jiayi Wen is an undergraduate student of Nanchang University. Her research focused on serum and urine samples treatment.
Yiyang Peng is an undergraduate student of Nanchang University. His research focused on synthesis of nano materials.

Dr. Xiaomin Tang received his bachelor’s degree (2002) from Jiangxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China and now works in the fourth affiliated hospital. His research focused on clinic analysis.

Prof. Ping Qiu received Ph.D. degree of Food Science from Nanchang University, China in 2006, and now working in the same university as a Group leader. Research areas include chemical sensors, nano enzyme, electroanalysis chemistry and chemometrics.
